era_context: The late 17th century: the Ottoman Empire faces increasing pressure from European powers; the Qing Dynasty consolidates control over China; Louis XIV's France dominates European politics; the Scientific Revolution accelerates; global maritime trade networks expand under European colonial powers; Peter I begins to assert influence in Russia.

events.title: Peter I becomes Tsar of Russia (joint rule with Ivan V)
events.description: Following the death of Tsar Feodor III, Peter I (Peter the Great) and his half-brother Ivan V became joint tsars under the regency of their sister Sophia Alekseyevna. Peter was only 10 years old at the time, but this event marked the beginning of his rise to power. Though Sophia held regency, Peter's eventual dominance would transform Russia into a major European power. The dual tsardom was an unusual arrangement that reflected the complex politics of the Russian court.

sources.name: Russian court records and the Kremlin archives
Historiographic consensus on Peter the Great's early life

events.certainty_note: Peter's accession to the throne is precisely documented in Russian records; the date of 1682 is well-established.

events.title: Streltsy Revolt (Streltsy uprising) in Moscow
events.description: The Streltsy (elite musketeers of the Russian army) revolted in Moscow in May 1682, protesting against their treatment and the political changes following Feodor III's death. The revolt was instigated partly by Sophia Alekseyevna's supporters and partly by genuine grievances among the Streltsy. The uprising resulted in the massacre of several nobles and the strengthening of Sophia's regency. This event demonstrated the volatility of Russian politics and the power of the military elite.

sources.name: Russian court records and eyewitness accounts

events.certainty_note: The Streltsy Revolt is well-documented in Russian sources; the date of May 1682 is confirmed by multiple contemporary accounts.

events.title: Louis XIV moves court to Versailles
events.description: King Louis XIV formally moved the royal court from Paris to the newly constructed Palace of Versailles in 1682. This move was both a practical and symbolic assertion of royal power, removing the court from the potentially rebellious capital and establishing a new center of royal authority. Versailles became the model for European absolutism and a symbol of Louis XIV's power and cultural dominance. The move also facilitated the king's control over the nobility by requiring them to reside at court.

sources.name: French court records and royal decrees
Memoirs of courtiers and contemporary accounts
Historiographic consensus on Louis XIV and Versailles

events.certainty_note: The move to Versailles is precisely documented; the year 1682 is confirmed as the date of the formal transfer of the court.

events.title: La Salle explores the Mississippi River and claims Louisiana for France
events.description: French explorer René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle, completed his expedition down the Mississippi River and reached the Gulf of Mexico in April 1682. He claimed the entire Mississippi River valley for France and named it 'Louisiana' in honor of King Louis XIV. This exploration established French claims to vast territories in North America and laid the groundwork for French colonial expansion in the continent.
key_figures: René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle (French explorer)

sources.name: La Salle's expedition records and reports to the French Crown
Historiographic consensus on La Salle's expedition

events.certainty_note: La Salle's expedition is well-documented; the date of April 1682 for reaching the Gulf of Mexico is confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Aurangzeb's military campaigns in the Deccan continue
events.description: Aurangzeb continued his aggressive military campaigns against the Maratha confederacy and other regional powers in the Deccan. The year 1682 saw ongoing military operations aimed at consolidating Mughal control over the region. These campaigns were costly and contributed to the strain on the Mughal imperial system, though they temporarily expanded Mughal territorial control.

sources.name: Mughal court records and imperial chronicles

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Mughal military campaigns in 1682 is well-documented, though specific battle dates vary across sources.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor consolidates Qing control over China
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor continued to consolidate Qing control over China in 1682. The suppression of the Three Feudatories (completed in 1681) had strengthened central authority, and 1682 saw the continuation of administrative reforms and the expansion of Qing cultural and political influence. The Kangxi Emperor's reign (1661–1722) was marked by relative stability and prosperity, establishing the Qing Dynasty as a major power.

sources.name: Qing official records and imperial chronicles
Historiographic consensus on the Kangxi Emperor

events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's consolidation of power is well-documented in Qing records; 1682 falls within a well-attested period of his reign.

disconfirming_evidence: The move of Louis XIV's court to Versailles is sometimes portrayed as an immediate and complete transfer of power; however, modern scholarship emphasizes that the transition was gradual and that Paris remained politically significant. Additionally, the cultural impact of Versailles on European absolutism, while real, is sometimes overstated in older historiography. Regarding La Salle's exploration, while his claim to Louisiana was significant, the actual French colonization of the region was slow and limited, and the claim was not effectively enforced until much later. The Streltsy Revolt is sometimes portrayed as a simple power grab by Sophia; recent scholarship suggests more complex motivations, including genuine grievances among the Streltsy and factional politics within the court.
historiographic_note: The year 1682 is well-documented in European and Chinese sources, particularly regarding the major political events (Peter's accession, Louis XIV's move to Versailles, La Salle's exploration). Russian sources are rich for the Streltsy Revolt and court politics. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; the experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite groups are underrepresented. Indigenous perspectives from North America regarding La Salle's exploration are sparse or absent from European records. The Mughal sources are court-centric and may underrepresent regional resistance and dissent. Bias toward written records means oral traditions and archaeological evidence are underutilized.

graph_edges.note: Peter's accession as tsar directly precipitated the Streltsy Revolt.
The Streltsy Revolt of 1682 set the stage for Peter's eventual consolidation of power in 1689.
The move to Versailles in 1682 was a defining moment in Louis XIV's reign and shaped French politics for the remainder of his rule.
La Salle's exploration and claim to Louisiana in 1682 laid the groundwork for French colonial expansion, including the founding of New Orleans.
1682 falls within the final decades of Aurangzeb's reign, a period of imperial overextension.
1682 falls within the Kangxi Emperor's reign, a period of Qing consolidation and stability.
